affected_groups: currently enrolled disadvantaged and Roma students
students in demographically contracting rural areas
future cohorts who would benefit from validated reform
policymakers balancing evidentiary and political risk
content.decision_question: Should Hungary delay national structural reform in favor of a regional pilot to build a stronger evidence base, accepting that current cohorts of disadvantaged students continue to experience documented segregation and inequality during the pilot period, or should it proceed with a national reform now despite acknowledged uncertainty about mechanism-specific effects and confounds?
content.evidence_boundary: Pilot results can clarify Hungary-specific causal effects of de-tracking, isolate confounds present in the Finnish historical case, and test whether zoning/choice controls are necessary complements — but evidence cannot determine how much weight to give the opportunity cost borne by current students during a multi-year pilot versus the risk of a poorly designed, precipitous national reform; that trade-off is a value judgment about acceptable transition costs.
content.tension: A pilot approach (T6) allows evidence to be generated before committing to national policy, reducing the risk of a costly, potentially non-representative or reversible national rollout. However, the underlying harms — extreme segregation, catastrophic Roma dropout rates, below-average disadvantaged proficiency — are already well documented and ongoing, and demographic contraction is actively intensifying urban concentration of elite tracks while cohorts shrink. Delaying national action to gather more evidence has its own real-world cost measured in current students' outcomes.
content.title: Evidence-gathering caution versus urgency of documented harm
value_poles: rigor and evidentiary caution before committing to reform
urgency of addressing ongoing, documented harm
